title: Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus
oneLiner: Autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells → absolute insulin deficiency; presents acutely with DKA or hyperglycaemia in young people; lifelong insulin required.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA):** Look for **ketones >3 mmol/L + pH <7.3 + bicarb <15** – this is not routine hyperglycaemia. Treat with fluids, insulin, K+ replacement immediately.
- **Severe hypoglycaemia:** In any known diabetic on insulin: **GCS <12 with blood glucose <3.0 mmol/L** – give IM glucagon 1 mg or IV 10% dextrose 100 mL over 10 minutes.
- **Cerebral oedema (in children with DKA):** **Rising GCS + bradycardia + hypertension** – emergency senior/ICU involvement; restrict fluids, consider mannitol.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Polyuria, polydipsia, nocturia
  - Unexplained weight loss (over weeks)
  - Fatigue, blurred vision
  - +/- symptoms of DKA: nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain, deep sighing (Kussmaul) respiration, fruity breath
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Dehydration (reduced skin turgor, dry mucous membranes, tachycardia)
  - Hypotension (if severe DKA)
  - Altered consciousness (GCS <15) – DKA or hypoglycaemia
  - Fruity breath / Kussmaul breathing (DKA)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Type 2 diabetes** – older, obese, gradual onset, no ketones, ± acanthosis nigricans, family history.
- **LADA (latent autoimmune diabetes in adults)** – >30 years, slow onset, initially non-insulin requiring, but positive autoantibodies.
- **Monogenic diabetes (e.g. MODY)** – strong family history (autosomal dominant), onset <25, not insulin-dependent initially, no autoantibodies.
- **Pancreatic diabetes** – history of pancreatitis, pancreatic surgery, cystic fibrosis.
- **Splitter:** **Presence of significant ketosis/ketones >1.0 mmol/L** points to T1DM/LADA/DKA, not T2DM.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Capillary blood glucose (CBG)
  - Urine or blood ketones (beta-hydroxybutyrate) – **mandatory** in any sick diabetic
  - ECG (hyperkalaemia in DKA; hypo in treatment)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, bicarbonate, blood gas (venous pH, pCO2, HCO3)
  - Glucose, HbA1c (baseline)
  - C-peptide (low/normal in T1, high in insulin resistance)
  - Autoantibodies: GAD, IA2, ZnT8 (for confirmation)
- **Imaging:** None for diagnosis. Consider CT/MRI brain if altered GCS not explained by glucose (rule out cerebral oedema/stroke).
- **Special tests:** OGTT rarely needed; formal insulin/glucose clamp only in research.
- **Diagnostic criteria (NICE/WHO):**
  - Fasting glucose ≥7.0 mmol/L
  - Random glucose ≥11.1 mmol/L + classic symptoms
  - HbA1c ≥48 mmol/mol (6.5%) – but acute illness may invalidate
- **Never-miss test:** **Blood ketones** in any hyperglycaemic patient with suspected T1DM – omission may miss DKA and delay life-saving insulin.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If DKA: start DKA protocol – 0.9% saline 1 L over 1 hour, fixed-rate insulin infusion 0.1 units/kg/hour (after confirming K+ >3.3 mmol/L), replace K+ as per local policy.
  - If severe hypo (BG <3.0): IM glucagon 1 mg or IV 10% dextrose 100 mL.
  - If new diagnosis, no DKA: start basal-bolus insulin (see Drugs).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Monitor CBG before meals + at bedtime + 2am (for basal assessment).
  - Adjust insulin doses based on blood glucose (target: pre-meal 5–8 mmol/L, bedtime 6–10 mmol/L in adults).
  - Teach insulin injection technique, sick day rules, hypoglycaemia management.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Refer to diabetes specialist nurse, dietitian, structured education (DAFNE for adults).
  - Annual reviews: HbA1c (<53 mmol/mol ideal), retinal screening, foot exam, renal function, BP, lipid profile.
- **Default Orders (if admitted, e.g. DKA):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% saline – first litre over 1 hour, then according to deficit + maintenance (watch for fluid shifts)
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH if DKA + immobility (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily)
  - Diet: NBM until stabilised, then start oral diet with insulin matching
  - Observations: monitor CBG hourly, NEWS2 every 1-2 hours initially; for DKA: also U&E and ketones 2-hourly
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line: Basal-bolus insulin regimen**
  - Basal: insulin glargine (Lantus) 0.2–0.3 units/kg once daily at same time; **starting dose – [check local policy]**
  - Bolus (prandial): insulin aspart (NovoRapid) or insulin lispro (Humalog) 0.1–0.15 units/kg with each meal; adjust based on carb counting and postprandial glucose
  - **Alternative:** insulin detemir (Levemir) as basal; insulin glulisine (Apidra) as bolus
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Premixed insulin (e.g. Humulin M3) – only if unable to manage basal-bolus
  - Insulin pump (CSII) – for selected patients (e.g. recurrent severe hypo, dawn phenomenon)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - No absolute contraindications to insulin; check for latex allergy in some prefilled pens
  - Beta-blockers mask hypoglycaemic symptoms; careful co-prescribing
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Renal impairment (eGFR <30):** reduce insulin dose (prolonged half-life); monitor glucose closely; avoid metformin (if mistakenly given for T2)
  - **Hepatic impairment:** no specific adjustment, but may increase hypoglycaemia risk
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Giving an IV bolus of insulin in DKA** (except in very rare paediatric protocols) – can cause fatal hypokalaemia; always use fixed-rate infusion.
  - **Under-replacing potassium** – K+ moves intracellularly with insulin; check K+ hourly and replace if <5.1 mmol/L.
  - **Overcorrecting hypoglycaemia** – giving too much dextrose/glucagon can cause rebound hyperglycaemia and osmotic diuresis.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) – hyperglycaemia, metabolic acidosis, potassium shifts
  - Severe hypoglycaemia – risk from insulin excess, missed meals, alcohol
  - Hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state (HHS) – uncommon in T1DM, but can occur with extreme dehydration
  - Cerebral oedema (children in DKA, especially if rapid fluid/insulin correction)
- **Chronic:**
  - Microvascular: diabetic retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy (sensory/motor/autonomic)
  - Macrovascular: accelerated atherosclerosis (MI, stroke, PAD)
  - Foot complications: ulcers, infection, Charcot joint; annual foot check essential
sections.Prognosis: - With modern insulin, basal-bolus regimens, and good glycaemic control, life expectancy is near-normal but reduced by ~10 years vs general population due to cardiovascular risk; complications reduce quality of life if HbA1c persistently >69 mmol/mol.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Check blood ketones in every unwell T1DM patient** – not just glucose. Ketones >1.5 mmol/L in illness → risk of DKA.
- **Teach sick day rules:** never stop insulin; increase basal dose by 10-20% if unwell; check ketones 4-hourly; attend ED if vomiting >2 doses of food/fluids.
- **Ensure every T1DM patient has a glucagon kit** prescribed and a family member trained to use it – discharge without one is a safety risk.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not delay insulin in DKA** – waiting for fluids to run first can prolong acidosis; start insulin simultaneously (once K+ >3.3).
- **Do not give metformin to T1DM patients** – it is not indicated and may increase risk of lactic acidosis in DKA.
- **Do not send a new T1DM home without specialist follow-up** – they need immediate diabetes team input for education, carb counting, and insulin adjustment.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Vomiting >2 episodes or unable to keep down food/fluids
  - Blood glucose persistently >15 mmol/L with ketones >1.5 mmol/L despite extra insulin
  - Severe hypoglycaemia (<3.0 mmol/L) not responding to oral treatment, or needing glucagon/paramedic help
  - Any new confusion, drowsiness, or rapid breathing
- **Follow-up:**
  - Diabetes specialist nurse phone call within 24-48 hours
  - Outpatient diabetes clinic within 2 weeks for new diagnosis; 3-6 monthly for established T1DM
  - Annual retinal screening (GP or optician) and foot exam (podiatrist or GP)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5
  - DKA with pH <7.1 after 2 hours of treatment (refractory DKA)
  - GCS <13 or drop of ≥2 points (suspected cerebral oedema in children)
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L (sepsis? mesenteric ischaemia? shock)
  - Severe hypoglycaemia not responding to IM glucagon + IV dextrose (need IV octreotide? rare)
  - Newly diagnosed child with T1DM – discuss with paediatric diabetes team immediately

rawMarkdown:
## Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus
**One-liner:** Autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells → absolute insulin deficiency; presents acutely with DKA or hyperglycaemia in young people; lifelong insulin required.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA):** Look for **ketones >3 mmol/L + pH <7.3 + bicarb <15** – this is not routine hyperglycaemia. Treat with fluids, insulin, K+ replacement immediately.
- **Severe hypoglycaemia:** In any known diabetic on insulin: **GCS <12 with blood glucose <3.0 mmol/L** – give IM glucagon 1 mg or IV 10% dextrose 100 mL over 10 minutes.
- **Cerebral oedema (in children with DKA):** **Rising GCS + bradycardia + hypertension** – emergency senior/ICU involvement; restrict fluids, consider mannitol.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Polyuria, polydipsia, nocturia
  - Unexplained weight loss (over weeks)
  - Fatigue, blurred vision
  - +/- symptoms of DKA: nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain, deep sighing (Kussmaul) respiration, fruity breath
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Dehydration (reduced skin turgor, dry mucous membranes, tachycardia)
  - Hypotension (if severe DKA)
  - Altered consciousness (GCS <15) – DKA or hypoglycaemia
  - Fruity breath / Kussmaul breathing (DKA)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Type 2 diabetes** – older, obese, gradual onset, no ketones, ± acanthosis nigricans, family history.
- **LADA (latent autoimmune diabetes in adults)** – >30 years, slow onset, initially non-insulin requiring, but positive autoantibodies.
- **Monogenic diabetes (e.g. MODY)** – strong family history (autosomal dominant), onset <25, not insulin-dependent initially, no autoantibodies.
- **Pancreatic diabetes** – history of pancreatitis, pancreatic surgery, cystic fibrosis.
- **Splitter:** **Presence of significant ketosis/ketones >1.0 mmol/L** points to T1DM/LADA/DKA, not T2DM.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Capillary blood glucose (CBG)
  - Urine or blood ketones (beta-hydroxybutyrate) – **mandatory** in any sick diabetic
  - ECG (hyperkalaemia in DKA; hypo in treatment)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, bicarbonate, blood gas (venous pH, pCO2, HCO3)
  - Glucose, HbA1c (baseline)
  - C-peptide (low/normal in T1, high in insulin resistance)
  - Autoantibodies: GAD, IA2, ZnT8 (for confirmation)
- **Imaging:** None for diagnosis. Consider CT/MRI brain if altered GCS not explained by glucose (rule out cerebral oedema/stroke).
- **Special tests:** OGTT rarely needed; formal insulin/glucose clamp only in research.
- **Diagnostic criteria (NICE/WHO):**
  - Fasting glucose ≥7.0 mmol/L
  - Random glucose ≥11.1 mmol/L + classic symptoms
  - HbA1c ≥48 mmol/mol (6.5%) – but acute illness may invalidate
- **Never-miss test:** **Blood ketones** in any hyperglycaemic patient with suspected T1DM – omission may miss DKA and delay life-saving insulin.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If DKA: start DKA protocol – 0.9% saline 1 L over 1 hour, fixed-rate insulin infusion 0.1 units/kg/hour (after confirming K+ >3.3 mmol/L), replace K+ as per local policy.
  - If severe hypo (BG <3.0): IM glucagon 1 mg or IV 10% dextrose 100 mL.
  - If new diagnosis, no DKA: start basal-bolus insulin (see Drugs).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Monitor CBG before meals + at bedtime + 2am (for basal assessment).
  - Adjust insulin doses based on blood glucose (target: pre-meal 5–8 mmol/L, bedtime 6–10 mmol/L in adults).
  - Teach insulin injection technique, sick day rules, hypoglycaemia management.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Refer to diabetes specialist nurse, dietitian, structured education (DAFNE for adults).
  - Annual reviews: HbA1c (<53 mmol/mol ideal), retinal screening, foot exam, renal function, BP, lipid profile.
- **Default Orders (if admitted, e.g. DKA):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% saline – first litre over 1 hour, then according to deficit + maintenance (watch for fluid shifts)
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH if DKA + immobility (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily)
  - Diet: NBM until stabilised, then start oral diet with insulin matching
  - Observations: monitor CBG hourly, NEWS2 every 1-2 hours initially; for DKA: also U&E and ketones 2-hourly

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line: Basal-bolus insulin regimen**
  - Basal: insulin glargine (Lantus) 0.2–0.3 units/kg once daily at same time; **starting dose – [check local policy]**
  - Bolus (prandial): insulin aspart (NovoRapid) or insulin lispro (Humalog) 0.1–0.15 units/kg with each meal; adjust based on carb counting and postprandial glucose
  - **Alternative:** insulin detemir (Levemir) as basal; insulin glulisine (Apidra) as bolus
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Premixed insulin (e.g. Humulin M3) – only if unable to manage basal-bolus
  - Insulin pump (CSII) – for selected patients (e.g. recurrent severe hypo, dawn phenomenon)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - No absolute contraindications to insulin; check for latex allergy in some prefilled pens
  - Beta-blockers mask hypoglycaemic symptoms; careful co-prescribing
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Renal impairment (eGFR <30):** reduce insulin dose (prolonged half-life); monitor glucose closely; avoid metformin (if mistakenly given for T2)
  - **Hepatic impairment:** no specific adjustment, but may increase hypoglycaemia risk
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Giving an IV bolus of insulin in DKA** (except in very rare paediatric protocols) – can cause fatal hypokalaemia; always use fixed-rate infusion.
  - **Under-replacing potassium** – K+ moves intracellularly with insulin; check K+ hourly and replace if <5.1 mmol/L.
  - **Overcorrecting hypoglycaemia** – giving too much dextrose/glucagon can cause rebound hyperglycaemia and osmotic diuresis.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) – hyperglycaemia, metabolic acidosis, potassium shifts
  - Severe hypoglycaemia – risk from insulin excess, missed meals, alcohol
  - Hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state (HHS) – uncommon in T1DM, but can occur with extreme dehydration
  - Cerebral oedema (children in DKA, especially if rapid fluid/insulin correction)
- **Chronic:**
  - Microvascular: diabetic retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy (sensory/motor/autonomic)
  - Macrovascular: accelerated atherosclerosis (MI, stroke, PAD)
  - Foot complications: ulcers, infection, Charcot joint; annual foot check essential

### Prognosis
- With modern insulin, basal-bolus regimens, and good glycaemic control, life expectancy is near-normal but reduced by ~10 years vs general population due to cardiovascular risk; complications reduce quality of life if HbA1c persistently >69 mmol/mol.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Check blood ketones in every unwell T1DM patient** – not just glucose. Ketones >1.5 mmol/L in illness → risk of DKA.
- **Teach sick day rules:** never stop insulin; increase basal dose by 10-20% if unwell; check ketones 4-hourly; attend ED if vomiting >2 doses of food/fluids.
- **Ensure every T1DM patient has a glucagon kit** prescribed and a family member trained to use it – discharge without one is a safety risk.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not delay insulin in DKA** – waiting for fluids to run first can prolong acidosis; start insulin simultaneously (once K+ >3.3).
- **Do not give metformin to T1DM patients** – it is not indicated and may increase risk of lactic acidosis in DKA.
- **Do not send a new T1DM home without specialist follow-up** – they need immediate diabetes team input for education, carb counting, and insulin adjustment.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Vomiting >2 episodes or unable to keep down food/fluids
  - Blood glucose persistently >15 mmol/L with ketones >1.5 mmol/L despite extra insulin
  - Severe hypoglycaemia (<3.0 mmol/L) not responding to oral treatment, or needing glucagon/paramedic help
  - Any new confusion, drowsiness, or rapid breathing
- **Follow-up:**
  - Diabetes specialist nurse phone call within 24-48 hours
  - Outpatient diabetes clinic within 2 weeks for new diagnosis; 3-6 monthly for established T1DM
  - Annual retinal screening (GP or optician) and foot exam (podiatrist or GP)

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5
  - DKA with pH <7.1 after 2 hours of treatment (refractory DKA)
  - GCS <13 or drop of ≥2 points (suspected cerebral oedema in children)
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L (sepsis? mesenteric ischaemia? shock)
  - Severe hypoglycaemia not responding to IM glucagon + IV dextrose (need IV octreotide? rare)
  - Newly diagnosed child with T1DM – discuss with paediatric diabetes team immediately
